# Research Institute Governance Structures

## Board Models

Research institutes typically operate under one of three board models: a charity trustee model, an advisory-council model, or a corporate subsidiary model. The trustee model places legal authority in a small group of volunteers responsible for fiduciary oversight. The advisory-council model uses a larger body with domain experts but vests legal authority in a CEO or director. The corporate subsidiary model embeds the institute inside a larger parent organisation.

## Programme Oversight

Effective institutes separate strategic oversight from day-to-day programme management. A governing board or council sets the portfolio priorities, allocates the endowment, and approves major contracts. Programme directors manage execution within those parameters, with agreed milestone checkpoints at which the board may redirect or close a programme.

## Staff and Director Roles

Senior directors typically hold responsibility for multiple programmes and report directly to the board. Junior investigators or fellows operate under a senior director's scientific supervision and may lead a single programme or work stream. This structure is common in applied-research organisations where a lead scientist carries both programmatic and commercial responsibility.

## Succession and Continuity

Governance best practice requires institutes to hold succession plans for all senior director roles. The plan documents who would assume day-to-day authority in the event of unexpected departure, the notice period required, and the handover timeline for active programme contracts.
